html:ul.html:li: Every connective has an arity, which is the number of sentences the connective takes to form a sentence.
A binary connective is a connective with an arity of 2.
A unary connective is a connective with an arity of 1.
A nullary connective is a connective with an arity of 0.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Not all connectives, however, are binary. We can have connectives that combine any number of sentences together. Some connectives might be ternary, for example, combining three sentences into one. Or some might be quaternary, combining four sentences into one. The number of sentences a connective combines is called the arity of the connective. Binary connectives, then, have an arity of 2; ternary connectives have an arity of 3; and so on.

In fact, though, we won't spend much time on connectives with arities higher than 2. In part, this is because these are just less used in lots of logic. In part, it's because there's not a super-convenient way to write them! With a binary connective, you can just put it between the things it combines, as we've done with \land and \lor . But that doesn't work as well for higher arities. (No problem putting them in trees, though; just use three branches for a ternary connective, four for a quaternary, and so on.) We will, though, spend a lot of time with connectives with lower arities: 1 and 0. These are called unary and nullary connectives, respectively.
Let me pause to lay out our first definition. Definitions are key in logic, and we'll meet a lot of them this semester.

html:ul.html:li: Every connective has an arity, which is the number of sentences the connective takes to form a sentence.
A binary connective is a connective with an arity of 2.
A unary connective is a connective with an arity of 1.
A nullary connective is a connective with an arity of 0.

fr:mainmatter.html:p:
Unary first. The unary connective we'll use a lot uses the symbol 
eg . This connective takes one component sentence to make a new sentence. I'll pronounce this "not", and call this connective "negation". Suppose we're working in a language with just the single atomic sentence p and the sole connective 
eg . Then we have all of the following as sentences: p, 
eg p, 
eg 
eg p, 
eg 
eg 
eg p, 
eg 
eg 
eg 
eg p, and so on. (Indeed, all the sentences in this language have some number (maybe 0) of negations followed by p.)

Note that we don't need any parentheses for these sentences. The sentence 
eg 
eg p applies 
eg to the sentence 
eg p; there's no other way to read it. However, when we start to combine unary and binary connectives, sometimes parentheses are still necessary. For example, consider a language with atomic sentences p, q and connectives \land , 
eg . Compare the two sentences (
eg p) \land q and 
eg (p \land q). The first of these uses \land to combine 
eg p with q; the second uses 
eg with the single sentence p \land q. Both are perfectly good sentences, but they are different, and the difference is important.

We can again see the difference using trees. Binary connectives still make two branches, and unary connectives make just a single branch. So these two sentences produce the following two trees. Make sure you pause to see which is which.

Sometimes, the number of parentheses we need can get a little bit out of control. For the most part, the answer is just practice: getting used to dealing with three or four parentheses deep is very doable, and you'll probably be totally fine even if you're just comfy with like two layers. However, there's a common convention I'll use to save on some parentheses, and you'll need to know how to read it.

The convention is this: unary connectives always attach to the smallest sentence they can. So if I write something like "
eg p \land q", it might seem ambiguous: is 
eg combining with just p, or with p \land q? Since p is smaller, though, the convention says that it combines with p. So 
eg p \land q is the very same sentence as (
eg p) \land q. If we want 
eg to combine with p \land q, then parentheses are needed: 
eg (p \land q).

When you write a sentence like (
eg p) \land q, you can include the parentheses or not; that's totally up to you, and either way is perfectly correct. But you'll need to know what I mean when I write something like 
eg p \land q: it's always (
eg p) \land q. And you'll need to include parentheses when what you mean is 
eg (p \land q); there's no other way to write this.
